notes.title: CIS Tender 9 Competitive Field — Three-Lens Analysis of Likely Winners

sections.heading: The T9 opportunity — corrected state math
sections.body: CIS Tender 9 opened 25 May 2026 with a 5 GW target. NSW is EXCLUDED (hit its CIS cap after T7); WA is not in the NEM and excluded. Effective eligible states: QLD, VIC, SA, TAS. State floors: VIC ≥1.6 GW guaranteed MINIMUM (with a 470 MW MAX cap on VIC solar-only, at Victoria's request); TAS ≥300 MW guaranteed MINIMUM. The ~3.1 GW contestable pool is open to QLD, SA, AND to VIC/TAS projects that clear their state minimums — so VIC and TAS wind can bid into TWO pools simultaneously. First Nations 500 MW set-aside for projects with ≥5% First Nations equity or revenue sharing. Target COD before end-2030. Bids close 20 July 2026, results Nov 2026. See docs/RESEARCH_CIS_T9_COMPETITIVE_FIELD.md for the full ~6,700-word analysis with tiered candidate tables.

sections.heading: The deliverability paradox — central frame
sections.body: T9 explicitly tightens MC2 (deliverability + timeline) and MC3 (organisational track record) per Clayton Utz (29 Aug 2025), Hamilton Locke (20 Aug 2025), Modo Energy (27 May 2026 — 'T9 shifts focus to deliverability'). Prior-round LOSERS look mature on paper (planning + connection progress) but carry 'shopped-and-didn't-convert' baggage. Prior-round WINNERS-BUT-STALLED are CISA-locked on the same project (see companion note 'CIS Rebid Rules Are Hardening — T9 Q&A Adds Formal Eligibility Bar' for the CIS T9 Q&A Item 8 eligibility mechanics). Fresh pipeline without prior-round exposure must independently demonstrate deliverability. Repeat winners with a delivery track record (RWE post-MacIntyre, Tilt post-Palmer FID) are structurally best-placed. SIDEBAR — the 'Zero for 15' problem: only 2 of ~69 CIS-awarded non-battery generation projects had reached construction by March 2026 (RE/wind-watch 30 Mar 2026). Palmer Wind (Tilt SA, 274 MW) reached FID 9 January 2026 with a 15-year AGL PPA — the first non-battery CIS generator to reach construction. The T9 assessor is designing around a catastrophic conversion problem.

sections.heading: Lens 1 — Prior CIS wind winners at rebid risk (via active withdraw-and-resubmit)
sections.body: Framing: prior CIS winners are EXCLUDED from rebidding T9 on the same project by default. CIS T9 Q&A Item 8 (published July 2026) hardens the pathway: projects awarded a CISA in any prior CIS tender that did NOT execute Project Documents are ineligible under Eligibility Criteria 11 UNLESS the proponent has (a) formally notified the Commonwealth in writing of withdrawal, OR (b) received written confirmation that negotiations have ceased. AEMO retains discretion to consider 'track record of not executing binding commitments' — an additional scoring penalty even for compliant withdrawals. Withdrawal may additionally constitute a Significant Event under the Proforma CISA (formal notification + remediation plan + prevention strategy). So the 'rebid' pathway is theoretically open but procedurally heavy. Wind projects most likely to attempt withdraw-and-resubmit: (1) Kentbruck Wind Farm (VIC, 600 MW, HMC Capital ex-Neoen) — 15+ months post-T1 award, still pre-FID, 'working through capital partnering' (RE 30 Mar 2026); most publicly stalled T1 wind winner. (2) Bell Bay Wind Farm (TAS, 224 MW, Equis, T4) — pre-FID mid-2026, construction targeted 2027; watch through Q1 2027. (3) Goyder North Stage 1 (SA, 300 MW, Neoen, T1) — FID expected 2026 per CER; if slipped, rebid credible. Notable NON-candidate: Hexham Wind (VIC, 600 MW, AGL, T4) — AGL publicly stated 'won't be in a position to reach FID until 2028' but is HOLDING (not withdrawing). Hexham stays CISA-locked; will not appear in T9 field. Lens 1 likely contributes ~0 to actual T9 outcomes given the procedural weight of the T9 Q&A Item 8 machinery.

sections.heading: Lens 2 — Gentailer + path-to-market (top 5)
sections.body: Historical CIS winners have been dominated by pure-play developers without integrated retail books to absorb the offtake. Deliverability lens: penalise developers with stalled prior awards; favour vertically-integrated gentailers with captive offtake demand. Structural finding: the gentailer T9-eligible wind/solar pipeline is genuinely thin. Origin has only Mortlake Battery (no wind/solar); EnergyAustralia only Loy Yang B BESS; CS Energy + Stanwell wind is all in construction (additionality-fail); Alinta hybrids have CODs 2030-12 and 2031-12 (beyond target). The STANDOUT gentailer pitch is AGL Barn Hill Wind (SA, 300 MW, EPBC-approved, AGL-owned since 2009) + co-located AGL Barn Hill BESS (270 MW) — the cleanest integrated wind+BESS + gentailer path-to-market bid in the entire T9 field. No ownership uncertainty, no acquisition dependency, advanced planning, single-owner integration — the archetype of what T9's tightened deliverability screen was designed to reward. Beyond Barn Hill: Nonowie Wind (SA 1,015 MW, Tilt — proven-delivery proxy post-Palmer); Iberdrola Three Rivers Solar (QLD 1,200 MW — global integrated utility); Guildford Wind (TAS 526 MW, Ark/Korea Zinc — industrial captive offtaker via Sun Metals); Alinta Yumali Wind Hybrid (SA 353 MW — gentailer but timing-wrong). Lens 2 likely: 1 confirmed (Barn Hill) + 1-2 possible.

sections.heading: Lens 3 — Earliest COD / MC2 credibility (top 5)
sections.body: Earliest COD + most advanced planning wins. MC2 (deliverability + timeline) dominates T9 scoring. A project bidding COD 2028 with EPBC approval structurally out-scores one bidding COD 2030 with only planning submitted. Top 5 by earliest-COD credibility (T9-eligible, non-locked, non-committed): (1) Fraser Coast Hybrid (QLD, 290 MW + 211 MWh, COD 2027-09-30, anticipated); (2) Northern Midlands Solar KCI (TAS, 310 MW, COD 2027-10-31, EPBC-submitted, anticipated — TAS state minimum candidate); (3) Solar River SB Project (SA, 256 MW + 650 MWh, COD 2027-11-29); (4) Colosseum Solar + BESS (QLD, 400 MW + 1,600 MWh, COD 2028-01-02 — strong 4hr storage pairing); (5) Mount Hopeful Wind (QLD, 370 MW, COD 2028-06-30, EPBC-APPROVED — best-in-class planning maturity). Earliest-COD picks skew heavily to QLD solar+BESS hybrids and mid-scale (250-400 MW) projects. Large-scale wind (>500 MW) generally comes with 2030+ CODs. The 5 GW target with a 2030 COD ceiling almost mechanically forces a mid-scale bias — DCCEEW may award 12-15 winners rather than 5-8.

sections.heading: The three-lens combined view — predicted T9 shape
sections.body: The three lenses are largely orthogonal — NO wind or solar project appears on all three, and very few appear on TWO. The T9 assessor is being asked to choose between competing values: rewarding second-chance rebids vs favouring gentailers vs favouring earliest-COD picks. Predicted T9 outcome shape: ~2-3 GW to mid-scale QLD Tier-1 candidates with EPBC + 2028-2029 COD (Lens 3 dominant pool — Callide, Mount Hopeful, Bulli Creek, Colosseum, Herries Range, Nyaninyuk, Tarong West); ~1 GW to proven-delivery proponents (Nonowie/Tilt, RWE fresh QLD wind, possibly Edify/Windlab fresh); ~500 MW First Nations set-aside (Ark Energy/Korea Zinc most likely); ~1.5-2 GW VIC state minimum + contestable overflow (Meering West OR Warracknabeal — only one wins — plus one small Acciona VIC pick); ~300-500 MW TAS state minimum (Guildford/Ark most likely); ~0 rebids from stalled prior CIS awards (T9 Q&A Item 8 machinery preserves scheme integrity); 1 confirmed + 1-2 possible gentailer wins (AGL Barn Hill is the anchor). T9 will look like T7 in shape — many mid-scale wins across many pure-play developers — with a modest deliverability premium favouring proven-track proponents over stalled or new-to-CIS candidates. The tightened MC2/MC3 changes WHY specific projects win, not fundamentally WHO wins; it will sharpen the pick order within the mid-scale QLD pool.

sections.heading: Standout pitch to watch — AGL Barn Hill Wind + BESS
sections.body: The single most-important T9 candidate to watch as a leading indicator of whether the gentailer premium is real or aspirational: AGL Energy's Barn Hill Wind (SA, 300 MW, EPBC-approved) paired with the co-located Barn Hill BESS (270 MW / 1,080 MWh). Seven signals: (1) 300 MW wind + 270 MW BESS co-located on a single SA site; (2) AGL has owned the wind project since 2009 — 17 years of ownership, no acquisition uncertainty; (3) wind EPBC-approved, BESS in planning-submitted; (4) direct integrated retail offtake path via AGL customer base; (5) fresh project — no prior CIS baggage, no MC3 drag; (6) cleanest wind+BESS pairing in the T9 field (system-benefit scoring premium); (7) structurally the ARCHETYPE of what T9's tightened deliverability screen was designed to reward. The T9 assessor gets to say 'we backed the gentailer archetype' without having to reach for weak candidates. If Barn Hill wins T9, it validates the deliverability tightening as more than rhetoric. If it doesn't win, it's a signal that pure-play developers with proven CIS track records (Tilt/Palmer archetype) still take the field even in a gentailer-friendly ruleset.

sections.heading: Cross-references and further reading
sections.body: See the companion note 'CIS Rebid Rules Are Hardening — T9 Q&A Adds Formal Eligibility Bar' (2026-07-07) for the detailed eligibility mechanics under CIS T9 Q&A Item 8 — that note is the authority on the withdraw-and-resubmit machinery. See 'CIS Project Status Deep Dive — Only BESS Has Progressed, Wind Still Stalled' (2026-06-27) for the underlying delivery-conversion data that makes 'Zero for 15' concrete. See 'Four CIS Wind Developers Told Government They Can't Deliver' (2026-07-08) for the Senate Estimates testimony (Brine/Phan/Wiltshire) confirming the scale of the wind delivery crisis. Full ~6,700-word analysis with tiered Table A (35 CISA-locked non-NSW projects) + Table B (tiered wind + large solar + solar+BESS competitive field) + state-by-state summary + developer signals + 11 'what you may not have considered' items + data-quality corrections + open questions: docs/RESEARCH_CIS_T9_COMPETITIVE_FIELD.md. In-app rendering as the T9 Competitive Field Deep Dive on the CIS T9 card in the Open Rounds CIS tab of the Scheme Tracker (shipped v3.21.2).

notes.title: Four CIS Wind Developers Told Government They Can't Deliver — The Full State of Play

sections.heading: The Senate Estimates Disclosure (Verified from Hansard)
sections.body: At the 2026-27 Budget Estimates hearing of the Senate Environment and Communications Legislation Committee (26 May 2026, pp.86-90), DCCEEW officials confirmed the following on the record. On wind project challenges, Mr Brine (Deputy Secretary): "The wind projects are the challenge. Batteries and solar are going great guns. It's the wind projects that are running into a range of social licence issues, transport issues and cost issues as the cost of turbines increases and the balance of plant costs increase. We're committed to working with those projects and doing what we can to find a way through. At the moment, we're hopeful we can resolve those issues and achieve the targets of the scheme." On the four projects, Ms Phan (Acting Branch Head, CIS Delivery and Governance): "Four projects have come to us — expressed at various times and of their own volition — to say that they've had challenges. And so, as Ms Wiltshire said, we've asked them for more information and are considering their request." On whether more would come forward, Ms Wiltshire (Acting Head, Clean Energy Investment and Facilitation Division): "We're not sure at this stage. As Ms Phan said, we don't solicit this information, but we do actively engage with all of our projects regularly, because it is part of their contractual obligations. We're not sure whether there'll be more at this stage." Program statistics confirmed in the same session: 94 projects announced as successful; 35 CISAs executed (with ~50 more under negotiation); 15 have reached final investment decision; 31 of the 94 are wind projects totalling 13.7 GW; approximately 6 milestone delays formally notified. On the financial close trajectory, Ms Wiltshire: "we expect around 15 gigawatts to have reached financial close by the end of this year [2026], but I don't have figures on the construction timeframes in front of me right now" — and the vast majority by end of 2027. Given only ~1.8 GW had reached FID earlier in the year and the wind sector headwinds documented in the same hearing, the 15 GW by end-2026 target looks extremely ambitious.

sections.heading: Which Four Projects? (NOT Confirmed in Hansard)
sections.body: The department explicitly declined to name the four projects. Ms Wiltshire: "One has been in the media, but no, there would be commercial sensitivity." When Senator Dean Smith pressed further — "I'm right to assume that you wouldn't give me the names of the four — or you can give me the names of the four?" — Ms Wiltshire replied that the names would be taken on notice, with the caveat that commercial sensitivity would constrain what could be provided. The AFR article (7 July 2026) identified Kentbruck (HMC Capital, ~750 MW, Victoria), Spicers Creek (Squadron Energy, ~600 MW, NSW), Valley of the Winds (ACEN, 1,600+ MW, NSW), and Thunderbolt (Neoen, ~720 MW, NSW) as the four — but these identifications are unverified media analysis, not confirmed in Hansard. The most-cited basis: all are CIS Tender 1 generation winners that locked in the lowest prices before construction cost inflation took hold, none had signed a CISA as of mid-2025, and all face the documented wind-sector headwinds Brine described. Junction Rivers (Windlab, 600 MW, NSW) is also cited as a fifth candidate. Treat the four specific names as informed speculation, not government-confirmed fact.

sections.heading: Why the Economics Don't Work
sections.body: The root problem is that CIS T1 wind projects bid in 2022-23, when construction cost assumptions were materially lower. Since then, onshore wind development costs have risen approximately 50% (RenewEconomy / Rystad Energy). The revenue floor in a CISA provides downside protection but does not increase to cover higher costs — it is fixed at the bid price. David Dixon (Rystad Energy) summarised the financing constraint: "The banks need the contracted revenues to cover their debt and their margins. The problem is that many of the projects have bid so low it is way below that." Without a PPA from a creditworthy offtaker (currently limited to Fortescue, Rio Tinto, and state utilities such as Synergy and SEC Victoria), the CISA revenue floor alone is insufficient for debt financing. Of 31 CIS-awarded wind projects, only three — Palmer, Waddi, and Carmody's Hill — have reached Final Investment Decision.

sections.heading: The NSW Lifeline Strategy
sections.body: The AFR reported on 7 July 2026 that struggling CIS wind developers are seeking NSW relief through the LTESA Round 8 tender (2.5 GW generation, bids due August 2026). The strategy: withdraw from CIS, rebid into LTESA at updated economics, and use the state Contract for Difference structure to unlock debt financing. NSW LTESA uses a CfD (floor and ceiling) vs the CIS pure revenue floor — a different risk profile that may provide stronger financing comfort. The NSW Government has established a "tiger team" in the energy department specifically to assist stalled wind projects. However, the CIS T9 Q&A (Item 8, July 2026) has now created a hard eligibility barrier: prior CIS award winners who did not execute a CISA are ineligible for CIS T9 unless they have formally notified the Commonwealth in writing of withdrawal. This means the rebid-into-LTESA strategy simultaneously locks them out of CIS T9 — a binary, irreversible choice.

sections.heading: Policy and Systemic Implications
sections.body: The concentration of failure in CIS T1 wind reflects a structural flaw in the auction design: price competition without adequate viability filters rewarded the most optimistic (or lowest-margin) bids. The Commonwealth has declined to renegotiate (Bowen: "we won't be renegotiating things that have previously been agreed") but has opened rebid pathways — now constrained by the T9 eligibility rules. Key risks for the 82% by 2030 target: (1) If the four-plus struggling T1 projects withdraw and fail to win LTESA, significant NSW generation capacity disappears permanently from the pipeline; (2) The CIS T9 window closes 20 July 2026 — projects that have not formally notified withdrawal before that date face both CIS ineligibility and missed LTESA timing; (3) Even projects that successfully rebid into LTESA face a construction timeline problem — any project starting later than end-2026 risks missing 2030 commercial operations. The NSW government recognises this; its tiger team and accelerated planning approvals are direct responses. On the CIS T9 / LTESA R8 overlap: LTESA and CIS rounds have historically been coordinated to avoid simultaneous open windows precisely to prevent the dual-optionality problem. This round is unusual in that both are open concurrently. However, there is an important practical mitigant: CIS T9 has no NSW generation allocation, and LTESA R8 is solely a NSW instrument — so in practice there is no direct overlap of capacity being competed for in the same jurisdiction at the same time. The mutual exclusivity issue is real in legal terms but the geographic separation reduces its practical bite. Policy advocacy positions that flow from this episode: (1) Jurisdictional coordination — the Commonwealth and NSW should formally confirm that future CIS and LTESA generation rounds will not be open simultaneously in the same state, eliminating the arbitrage window and the need for proponents to make an irreversible binary choice under time pressure; (2) Withdrawal transparency — any proponent formally withdrawing from a previously announced CIS award should be required to disclose that withdrawal publicly (not just to the Commonwealth) prior to the next round opening, so that the market knows the effective capacity available; (3) Capacity fate disclosure — when a project withdraws, the Commonwealth should clearly indicate whether the withdrawn MW are being retendered in the upcoming round, a future round, or are not being replaced, so that state governments, transmission planners, and the market can plan accordingly rather than operating on incomplete information about the effective pipeline.

notes.title: CIS Rebid Rules Are Hardening — T9 Q&A Adds Formal Eligibility Bar

sections.heading: The Three-Stage Tightening (Six Weeks)
sections.body: Restrictions on rebidding by prior CIS winners have evolved rapidly across three signals in approximately six weeks: (1) LTESA R8 Q&A, Item 19 (circa May 2026) — explicitly permissive: "A Project that has been awarded a CISA, but has not executed it, is eligible to participate this Tender Round." No formal withdrawal required. No mention of AEMO discretion or track record. The only restriction was on executed agreements — unexecuted awards carried no restriction at all. (2) Minister Bowen press conference, 24 June 2026 — "If a company is not able to comply with what it told the Government it would do when it won its auction, it is welcome to withdraw and bid again with new economics, and we will consider that" — still permissive, but introduced the concept of withdrawal as a precondition. (3) CIS Tender 9 Q&A, Item 8 (published July 2026 during the live T9 tender window) — hardest language yet: formal written withdrawal required as an eligibility condition, with AEMO track record discretion and Significant Event implications.

image.alt: LTESA R8 Q&A Item 19 — CIS winners eligibility
image.caption: LTESA R8 Q&A Item 19: explicitly states that a project awarded a CISA but has not executed it is eligible to participate. No formal withdrawal required.

sections.heading: What the CIS T9 Q&A Actually Says
sections.body: The LTESA R8 Q&A (Item 19) set the baseline: Section 4.1.1.4 prohibits holding both an executed LTESA and an executed CISA simultaneously. Section 5.36 adds that an executed agreement must be terminated before Bid Submission. But an unexecuted CISA award creates no barrier — eligible, full stop. The CIS T9 Q&A (Item 8) is a different legal universe: projects awarded a CISA in any prior CIS tender that did NOT execute Project Documents are ineligible for T9 under Eligibility Criteria 11, unless (a) the proponent has formally notified the Commonwealth in writing of withdrawal, or (b) received written confirmation from the Commonwealth that negotiations have ceased. AEMO may also consider track record of not executing binding commitments when developing Recommended Bids. Withdrawal may additionally constitute a Significant Event under the Proforma CISA — with formal notification, remediation plan, and prevention strategy obligations — and independent legal advice is recommended.

image.alt: CIS Tender 9 Q&A Item 8 — Rebid Projects eligibility criteria
image.caption: CIS T9 Q&A Item 8: prior CISA award winners ineligible for T9 under Eligibility Criteria 11 unless formally withdrawn in writing. AEMO track record discretion applies. Published on ASL tender portal during the live T9 window (closes 13 July 2026).

sections.heading: The Catch: Formal Written Notification Required
sections.body: The LTESA R8 Q&A created a clear arbitrage: hold an unexecuted CIS award, bid into LTESA, and decide later which to execute. No formal steps required. The CIS T9 Q&A closes that arbitrage by making the formal written notification a prerequisite for re-entry. Being in commercial discussions with LTESA, publicly signalling intent to withdraw, or simply not progressing a CIS project does not satisfy the test. A proponent that read the LTESA R8 Q&A, noted the permissive language, and began preparing an LTESA bid without sending a formal written withdrawal notice to the Commonwealth is now reading the CIS T9 eligibility criteria with some alarm. The question is how many of the 64% of CIS award winners who never executed their CISA are in exactly this position.

sections.heading: Interpretation and Implications
sections.body: The tightening from the LTESA Q&A through to the CIS T9 Q&A has occurred in approximately six weeks, suggesting the Government has been responding to observed market behaviour — proponents hedging across both schemes — and developing its formal response reactively. Key implications: (1) Not allowed to bid both — mutual exclusivity now has procedural teeth via Eligibility Criteria 11; (2) The Significant Event machinery is far heavier than Bowens "welcome to withdraw and rebid" framing implied. Under the Proforma CISA, a Significant Event requires: formal written notice to the Commonwealth, a remediation plan outlining how the issue will be fixed, and a prevention strategy documenting how similar events will be avoided. Voluntary withdrawal triggering this process is awkward by design; (3) AEMO track record discretion adds a further scoring penalty even for formally compliant withdrawals; (4) Proponents that acted on the LTESA R8 Q&A permissive language (unexecuted award = no barrier) without formal notification are now in a more difficult position; (5) Further tightening in T10 and beyond is likely as the Government continues to close the arbitrage window.

notes.title: Capricorn BESS Called In by QLD Deputy Premier — $500M+ Investment at Risk

sections.heading: What Happened
sections.body: The Queensland LNP government called in the Capricorn BESS (Potentia Energy, 300 MW / 1,200 MWh, Central Queensland) for state-level review in early 2026. Deputy Premier Steven Miles intervened, creating planning uncertainty for a project that had won CIS Tender 3 and was targeting construction in H2 2026 with commercial operations mid-2028. Estimated investment exceeds $500M.

sections.heading: Broader Impact
sections.body: The call-in has 'rattled the QLD BESS pipeline' according to Energy News Bulletin. Queensland's political environment for large-scale renewable projects has become less predictable under the LNP government elected in late 2025. This creates sovereign risk for CIS-contracted projects in Queensland — developers may prefer NSW, VIC, or SA where planning processes are more predictable.

sections.heading: CIS Program Implications
sections.body: Capricorn is a CIS Tender 3 winner. If the call-in results in refusal or material delay, it would be the second CIS project to face effective termination (after Junction Rivers, which failed to secure REZ access). Both failures are in Queensland. This pattern may influence future CIS tender allocations — the federal government may reduce QLD's CIS share if the state planning environment is seen as hostile.

notes.title: CIS Project Status Deep Dive — Only BESS Has Progressed, Wind Still Stuck

sections.heading: Verified Status (June 2026)
sections.body: Deep dive across 22 major CIS/LTESA projects confirms: only battery projects have meaningfully progressed to construction/commissioning. Liddell BESS (AGL, 500 MW) is commissioning with full ops expected June 2026. Four more BESS under construction: Tomago (AGL, 500 MW, broke ground Dec 2025), Calala (Equis, 250 MW, FC Mar 2025), Wooreen (EnergyAustralia, 350 MW, FC Feb 2025), Reeves Plains (Alinta/Sembcorp, 250 MW, FID Jul 2025). Goulburn River Solar (Lightsource bp, 585 MW) is the sole generation project in construction.

sections.heading: Wind Finally Moving — Slowly
sections.body: Palmer Wind Farm (Tilt, 288 MW, SA) reached FID in January 2026 — the first CIS-winning wind project to do so, over 2 years after CIS Tender 1 results. Goyder North (Neoen, 300+ MW, SA) received EPBC approval Feb 2026 and is targeting mid-2026 construction start. But the large GW-scale wind projects (Liverpool Range, Yanco Delta, Bungaban, Theodore, Valley of the Winds) are all still pre-FID with FIDs expected late 2026 through 2028.

sections.heading: At-Risk Projects
sections.body: Junction Rivers (Windlab, 600 MW wind + 200 MW BESS, NSW) failed to secure South-West REZ access rights — described as 'likely terminal'. Could be the first project dropped from CIS. Thunderbolt Wind Farm (Neoen, NSW) has reportedly not signed its CIS contract — status uncertain. Kentbruck (HMC Capital, VIC) has ministerial approval but HMC has had 'significant management and financial turmoil' and no FID confirmed.

notes.title: CIS Tender 8 Dispatchable — 4.2 GW / 16.1 GWh Awarded to 15 BESS Projects

sections.heading: Results Overview
sections.body: CIS Tender 8 (NEM dispatchable) awarded 4.2 GW / 16.1 GWh across 15 standalone lithium-ion battery projects on 24 June 2026 — the largest dedicated BESS tender in CIS history. All lithium-ion; pumped hydro and CAES received no awards. 70+ bids totalling 76.4 GW were received (~19x oversubscribed). Estimated $6B private capital, 6,800+ jobs.

sections.heading: QLD Dominance
sections.body: Queensland received 7 of 15 projects and 51% of capacity (2,150 MW) — a reversal from earlier rounds where NSW led. This reflects QLD's large BESS pipeline and connection availability, but raises the question of whether this further saturates an already-compressed QLD market. The irony: CIS is underwriting BESS in the state where merchant BESS revenue has collapsed 73%.

sections.heading: 4-Hour Duration Confirmed
sections.body: 13 of 15 projects are 4-hour duration, confirming 4-hour as the NEM default. Only Bulabul 1 (2-hour, Ampyr) and Woonga Creek (3.5-hour, Lightsource bp) deviate. The 2-hour playbook is effectively dead for new CIS bids.

sections.heading: Financial Close Gap
sections.body: Only Bulabul 1 (Ampyr, 300 MW / 600 MWh) had reached financial close at time of award. Across all CIS rounds, only 1.8 GW of 17.5 GW awarded has reached FID — a 10% conversion rate. The CIS is increasingly a scheme that awards capacity that does not get built.

notes.title: Zero for Fifteen — No CIS Wind Farm Has Started Construction

sections.heading: The Headline Stat
sections.body: As of March 2026, zero of 15 CIS-awarded wind farms had begun construction. Only 3 wind projects (Palmer, Waddi, Carmody's Hill) had reached any form of investment decision. Of 69 CIS-awarded projects across all rounds totalling ~20 GW, only 14 projects (3.3 GW) had begun construction — and these are overwhelmingly batteries, not wind or solar.

sections.heading: Why Wind Is Stuck
sections.body: Wind projects face a compounding problem: construction costs are up ~50% since 2020, forward electricity prices have dropped from ~$103/MWh to ~$80/MWh, and CIS revenue floors alone are insufficient for debt financing — projects also need PPAs with retailers, and corporate PPA volumes have been limited to 1-1.5 GW/year. Origin's Frank Calabria: 'Our problem is not ambition... The problem is economics — and it is getting worse, not better.'

sections.heading: CISA Signing Rate
sections.body: Senate Estimates (December 2025) revealed that of approximately 18 GW in successful CIS projects, only approximately 6.5 GW had actually signed CISAs — roughly 36%. An awarded-but-unsigned project has no binding Commonwealth obligation, making withdrawal straightforward.

notes.title: Minister Bowen Confirms CIS Winners Can Withdraw and Rebid — But Won't Renegotiate

sections.heading: The Key Quotes
sections.body: At the CIS Tender 8 results announcement (24 June 2026), Minister Bowen addressed the withdrawal question directly: 'If a company is not able to comply with what it told the Government it would do when it won its auction, it is welcome to withdraw and bid again with new economics, and we'll consider that.' But: 'No, we won't be doing that' — when asked about renegotiating existing CIS contracts.

sections.heading: The CIS-to-LTESA Arbitrage
sections.body: NSW LTESA Tender 8 (2.5 GW generation, launched mid-2026) is the first generation LTESA since CIS began — creating a potential arbitrage. Projects that never signed a CISA (64% of awards) can walk away easily. A CIS winner that withdraws could rebid into the LTESA with updated economics. LTESA uses a Contract for Difference structure vs CIS revenue floors — different risk profiles that may suit different project economics.

sections.heading: Mutual Exclusivity Rule
sections.body: Projects cannot hold both an LTESA and a CISA simultaneously. Bidders into NSW LTESA T8 must commit to forgoing CIS support. The mutual exclusivity creates a binary choice: stay with CIS at potentially unworkable economics, or withdraw and compete again in LTESA with no guarantee of success.

sections.heading: Withdrawal Candidates
sections.body: CIS Tender 1 wind projects are the most likely withdrawal candidates: Kentbruck (HMC Capital — 'significant management and financial turmoil'), Spicers Creek (Squadron Energy — 'materially higher construction costs'), Valley of the Winds (ACEN — planning delays), Thunderbolt (Neoen — reportedly not signed CISA). The risk for the Commonwealth is that its best NSW generation projects defect to the state scheme.

notes.title: BESS Revenue Hits All-Time Record Low — $29k/MW/yr in May 2026

sections.heading: The Number
sections.body: May 2026 NEM-wide battery revenue fell to $29k/MW/yr — the lowest monthly figure since Modo Energy began tracking in July 2022. This follows March 2026 hitting $44k/MW/yr (previous record low) and a brief January bounce ($124k/MW/yr from heatwave spikes). The Oct 2025 — Mar 2026 average of $73k/MW/yr is down 38% YoY.

sections.heading: What Is Driving This
sections.body: Two forces compounding simultaneously: (1) Mild peak demand narrowing energy spreads across the mainland — NEM-wide price spreads fell from $183/MWh to $121/MWh (34% compression); (2) Continued battery deployment increasing competition for a limited pool of merchant value — the fleet more than doubled from 2 GW to 8 GW in 12 months.

sections.heading: Financial Close Implications
sections.body: New BESS projects need to model revenue at $50-60k/MW/yr or lower for base-case financial close. Projects relying on $100k+ revenue assumptions from 2024 vintage forecasts face material downside risk. The Calala BESS ($260m non-recourse senior debt, Equis) and Bungama BESS (Amp Energy, fully merchant bank-financed) show that lenders are still willing — but at lower return expectations.

notes.title: Revenue Cannibalisation — No Floor in Sight

sections.heading: The Self-Defeating Dynamic
sections.body: WattClarity has coined the term 'battery correlation penalty' to describe the structural problem: all batteries charge during the midday solar trough, all discharge during the evening peak. This highly correlated operating pattern erodes the very spread they target. As more batteries connect, the pattern strengthens and spreads compress further.

sections.heading: Evidence of Acceleration
sections.body: QLD energy-only revenues dropped 73% as BESS capacity grew 2.7x. QLD 2-hour price spreads hit $78/MWh in Q1 2026, down from $244/MWh a year earlier — the 'lowest in a number of years'. Revenue dropped from $86k (Oct 2025) to $29k (May 2026) with no sign of stabilisation. Commodity price declines (gas -9-17% YoY, coal -12%) compound by lowering the evening price ceiling.

sections.heading: Potential Stabilisers
sections.body: Coal retirements are the key potential circuit-breaker. Eraring (2.88 GW) closing late 2027 and Yallourn (1.48 GW) mid-2028 will remove baseload, restoring some price volatility. Data centre demand growth (4x by 2030) and SA industrial expansion (doubling 2029-2034) will increase overall load. But timing matters — 2026-2027 may be the trough before coal retirements take effect.

sections.heading: Modo's Long-Term View
sections.body: Capture rates forecast to fall from 85% in 2026 to 58% by 2035. Lifetime average revenue for a 4-hour battery starting 2026: $263k/MW/yr (QLD leads at $281k/MW/yr). Energy arbitrage will drive >95% of revenue as FCAS saturates. The business model is shifting from high-margin merchant opportunism to contracted infrastructure with utility-like returns.

notes.title: Batteries Now Set NEM Marginal Price in 32% of All Intervals

sections.heading: The Landmark Quarter
sections.body: In Q1 2026, batteries overtook hydro as the most frequent price-setting technology in the NEM, setting marginal prices in 32% of all trading intervals. During evening peaks the figure rises to 50% (twice prior year), and during midday solar surplus periods batteries are the price-setter in 61% of intervals.

sections.heading: The Spread Compression Mechanism
sections.body: Battery discharge reached 359 MW average in Q1 2026 (up from 98 MW in Q1 2025 — 3.7x increase). This pushes evening peak prices DOWN. Simultaneously, battery charging increased by 872 MW on average, pushing midday prices UP. The net effect: the price spread between midday trough and evening peak is compressing from both sides. NEM-wide spreads fell from $183/MWh to $121/MWh.

sections.heading: Gas and Coal Displaced
sections.body: Gas generation fell to its lowest level since 1999. Coal generation declined 4.4% in Q1 2026. Gas peakers are being displaced from their traditional evening price-setting role. This is a structural shift — batteries are not just participants in the NEM, they ARE the market.

notes.title: Queensland — The Canary for NEM-Wide Revenue Collapse

sections.heading: The Collapse
sections.body: Queensland went from the NEM's top-earning BESS state to its worst-performing in a single year. Energy-only revenues dropped 73% YoY. Q1 2026 average price spreads fell 68% to $78/MWh from $244/MWh. BESS capacity grew 2.7x. Duration barely changed (+4%), offering no offset.

sections.heading: The Paradox
sections.body: Despite the lowest absolute revenue, QLD batteries actually recorded the highest capture rates across the fleet in Q1 2026 — supported by consistent (if small) arbitrage conditions. This means QLD operators are technically performing well — they're just operating in a market where well-performing still means poor returns.

sections.heading: Leading Indicator
sections.body: NSW and VIC are 12-18 months behind QLD on the cannibalisation curve. The physics of arbitrage are the same everywhere — as each state adds capacity, the same compression pattern will repeat. NSW energy revenue already halved YoY. The only question is timing, not direction.

notes.title: South Australia — The Resilient Outlier (But For How Long?)

sections.heading: Revenue Up 49% YoY
sections.body: While every other state saw revenue decline, SA battery revenue climbed 49% YoY (Oct 2025 - Mar 2026). Q1 2026 price spreads increased to $328/MWh — more than 4x QLD's $78/MWh. SA is the only state where batteries are still making genuinely attractive returns.

sections.heading: The August 2025 Jackpot
sections.body: In August 2025, SA batteries earned $563k/MW/yr, with 84.5% from Lower 1-second FCAS. The Heywood interconnector outage forced local FCAS procurement, creating extreme scarcity pricing. Hornsdale Power Reserve recorded its third-highest monthly revenue ever. Some SA batteries derived over 50% of annual revenue from FCAS.

sections.heading: Why It Won't Last
sections.body: SA's resilience is driven by limited interconnection — essentially island-like dynamics during constraint events. But with fleet growth, SA FCAS scarcity is 'unlikely to be repeated in 2026' according to Modo. The Bungama and Whyalla batteries will add 450 MW of competition. SA is not exempt from cannibalisation — it's just delayed.

notes.title: NEM Battery Fleet Doubles in 12 Months — 8 GW / 16.6 GWh

sections.heading: The Scale of Deployment
sections.body: The NEM commissioned 4.9 GWh of BESS in 2025 alone — matching the entire cumulative output from 2017 to 2024 combined. The fleet grew from 2 GW at the start of 2025 to 8 GW by March 2026 — 53 registered utility-scale units totalling 16.6 GWh. Battery daily load-shifting tripled in Q1 2026 vs prior year.

sections.heading: Key 2025 Commissionings
sections.body: Major batteries that came online: Waratah Super Battery (850 MW / 1,680 MWh, NSW — partial Aug 2025), Eraring Battery Stage 1 (460 MW / 1,770 MWh, NSW — Dec 2025), Melbourne RE Hub A3 (200 MW / 800 MWh, VIC — Dec 2025, NEM's first 4-hour battery), Western Downs Stages 1-2 (450 MW, QLD), Bouldercombe (200 MW, QLD), Swanbank (125 MW, QLD), Blyth (100 MW, SA).

sections.heading: The Pipeline Behind It
sections.body: 67.3 GW of battery storage is now progressing through the NEM connection process — up 33% from 50.5 GW a year ago. 33.2 GW is standalone battery capacity. Under construction: 7.7 GW. Modo projects 16.8 GW online by end 2027. AEMO's 2026 ISP targets 24 GW by 2030 — 9 GW higher than the 2024 ISP.

notes.title: 4-Hour Batteries Are Now Minimum Viable — 2-Hour Economics Structurally Challenged

sections.heading: The Duration Shift
sections.body: Melbourne RE Hub A3 (commissioned Dec 2025) is the NEM's first 4-hour battery and is showing early signs of superior spread capture compared to the 2-hour fleet. Modo projects lifetime average revenue of $263k/MW/yr for 4-hour systems (QLD leads at $281k/MW/yr). IRR for 4-hour: 8-14.5% depending on scenario. For 2-hour: below 10%, central scenario 5.5% or less.

sections.heading: Why Duration Matters Now
sections.body: 4-hour systems have 10-15% lower per-kWh costs (power electronics spread over more hours), double the capacity factor (16.7% vs 8.3%), and capture value in time periods that 2-hour batteries cannot reach. As 2-hour batteries saturate the evening peak, 4-hour systems can discharge into later evening and overnight periods where competition is thinner.

sections.heading: 8-Hour Emerging
sections.body: Richmond Valley BESS (275 MW / 2,200 MWh, 8-hour) is under construction for 2027 delivery — the longest duration grid battery in the NEM. NSW LTESA Round 6 awarded three long-duration projects totalling 1.17 GW / 11.98 GWh. The question: does extreme duration capture overnight and multi-day volatility, or does it over-build for events that rarely materialise?

notes.title: FCAS Revenue Structurally Saturated — Now Only 9% of Total BESS Revenue

sections.heading: The Decline
sections.body: FCAS now accounts for only 9% of total NEM BESS revenue — down from approximately 30% in 2023. Saturation occurred from late 2023 as a growing number of new entrants steadily eroded what had been a reliable revenue source. NSW and VIC FCAS revenues are now 'negligible'. Q1 2026 FCAS share collapsed to just 3% of total revenue.

sections.heading: Frequency Performance Payments — Negligible So Far
sections.body: The new Frequency Performance Payments (FPP) scheme launched 8 June 2025, replacing old Causer Pays allocation. Total FPP revenue June-December 2025 was only $2.14M across the entire fleet — peaking at $460k in October 2025. At this scale, FPP is a rounding error on battery economics.

sections.heading: Future Revenue Stack
sections.body: By 2030, Modo projects that >80% of battery revenues will come from energy arbitrage. FCAS can still contribute 20-30%+ during constraint events in SA and QLD, but these are episodic windfalls, not bankable base revenue. The revenue stack for new projects: contracted base (CIS/LTESA/virtual toll) + energy arbitrage + minor FCAS. The days of FCAS as a primary revenue driver are over.

notes.title: Autobidder Convergence — Trading Platform Choice Matters Less Than Expected

sections.heading: The Platforms
sections.body: Five main platforms compete in the NEM: Tesla Autobidder (~500 MW), Fluence Mosaic (~300 MW, also manages ~2,500 MW of wind/solar), Wartsila Intellibidder (~200 MW), Habitat Energy (UK-origin, Melbourne office, ~100 MW), and in-house systems (Neoen, AGL, Origin — ~2,000+ MW). Third-party optimisers manage over 800 MW of BESS.

sections.heading: The Surprising Finding
sections.body: Modo Energy's key finding: 'Frequent rebidding does not result in higher capture rates.' Revenue optimisation relies on forecast accuracy, not rebid frequency. Assets using the same autobidder can have very different results, indicating site-specific factors (location, duration, network constraints, portfolio effects) dominate over platform choice.

sections.heading: Bid Volume Explosion
sections.body: Daily energy bids in the NEM increased from 5,000/day in 2021 to 30,000/day in 2025 — driven overwhelmingly by battery autobidders submitting revised bids hundreds of times per day. As Fluence's Matt Grover put it: 'It has become an accepted truth in the industry that you'd be crazy to try and bid a battery into the market without some kind of automation.' 35% of grid-scale wind and solar also adopted automated bidding within the past two years.

notes.title: AEMO Directs Torrens Island Battery — Grid Security vs Battery Economics

sections.heading: What Happened
sections.body: AEMO directed AGL's Torrens Island BESS (250 MW / 250 MWh) in November 2025 and again in March 2026, constraining its operations for grid security reasons. This reduced the battery's revenue — despite AGL reporting $28M EBITDA in its first 9 months of operation.

sections.heading: The Precedent Risk
sections.body: Compensation for 'out of market actions' remains uncertain under NEM rules. If AEMO can direct battery operations without adequate compensation, this creates a material regulatory risk for battery investors. The Blyth BESS in SA lost approximately 12.7% of its annual discharge to curtailment in 2025.

sections.heading: Fleet-Wide Curtailment
sections.body: Total battery curtailment across the fleet was estimated at approximately 90,929 MWh in 2025. As the fleet grows and system security interactions become more complex, the tension between AEMO's reliability mandate and battery operators' commercial interests will intensify.

notes.title: CIS Tender 8 Seeks 16 GWh of Storage Across NEM

sections.heading: Tender Overview
sections.body: CIS Tender 8 is open and seeking 16 GWh of storage capacity across the NEM — the largest single storage procurement round under the Commonwealth CIS program to date. Results are expected mid-2026.

sections.heading: Strategic Questions
sections.body: A key unresolved policy question: can unsigned CIS winners from earlier rounds bid into current LTESA rounds? If allowed, this gives CIS winners dual-contract optionality — de-risk with CIS, then optimise with LTESA. If disallowed, it protects LTESA round integrity. No definitive public guidance has been found.

sections.heading: CIS T3 Benchmark
sections.body: CIS Tender 3 awarded 4.13 GW / 15.37 GWh of BESS — the largest battery tender in Australian history. This established the CIS as the primary mechanism for underwriting new BESS investment. Approximately one-third of battery capacity reaching financial close now uses government contracts (CIS, LTESA, VRET), transferring revenue risk onto public balance sheets.

notes.title: NSW LTESA Round 6 Awards 1.17 GW / 11.98 GWh — Long-Duration Focus

sections.heading: The Awards
sections.body: NSW LTESA Round 6 awarded three long-duration storage projects: Neoen Great Western (330 MW / 3,500 MWh), BW ESS Bannaby (233 MW / 2,676 MWh), and Ark Energy Bowmans Creek (250 MW / 2,414 MWh). Combined: 1.17 GW / 11.98 GWh. All are 8-10 hour duration — a clear signal that NSW is pivoting beyond the 2-hour playbook.

sections.heading: Implications for Revenue Cannibalisation
sections.body: Longer-duration batteries partially escape the 'battery correlation penalty' by dispatching into later evening and overnight periods where competition is thinner. If the 2-hour market is saturated, 4-8 hour systems capture different time windows. But if extreme duration is over-built, even these spreads will compress — it's a question of when, not if.

notes.title: The BESS Business Model Is Shifting — From Merchant Opportunism to Contracted Infrastructure

sections.heading: The Old Model (2017-2024)
sections.body: Early batteries like Hornsdale and Victorian Big Battery were high-margin, opportunistic assets. Revenue came from energy arbitrage spikes and FCAS scarcity — $100-150k+/MW/yr was achievable. Investors accepted merchant risk because returns were exceptional.

sections.heading: The New Model (2025+)
sections.body: Approximately 50% of offtake arrangements are now structured as virtual tolls, revenue-share, capacity swap arrangements, or firmed PPAs — replacing physical tolls as the dominant structure. One-third of battery capacity uses government contracts (CIS, LTESA, VRET). Commercial banks now finance purely merchant BESS (Amp Energy's Bungama was first fully merchant bank-financed). But returns have compressed from >15% to 8-14% IRR for 4-hour systems.

sections.heading: What This Means for Developers
sections.body: Developers are stacking 'riskier offtakes alongside government support arrangements' (Allens). Financiers are 'becoming more willing to provide attractive project finance debt sizing where there is a degree of sub-investment grade offtakes and some level of merchant exposure' (Macquarie). The era of $150k+/MW/yr merchant revenue is over. The era of contracted infrastructure returns is beginning — lower return, lower risk, essential service.

notes.title: NEM Wholesale Prices in Structural Decline — VIC Hits $35.68/MWh in April

sections.heading: The Numbers
sections.body: NEM wholesale averaged approximately $73/MWh in Q1 2026, down 12% YoY. But April 2026 was brutal: Victoria collapsed to $35.68/MWh (-52.3% YoY), SA fell to $56.18/MWh (-36.7% YoY), and the NEM average dropped 37.8% YoY. Forward prices are trading 12-18% below long-term averages.

sections.heading: Structural Drivers
sections.body: Gas generation at lowest level since 1999. Coal generation down 4.4% in Q1 2026. Grid-scale and distributed solar both set quarterly generation records. Rooftop solar growing +8.1% YoY. Gas prices down 9-17% YoY and coal down 12% — the evening price ceiling is lowering from the commodity side as well as the supply side.

sections.heading: Impact on BESS
sections.body: Lower wholesale prices directly compress BESS arbitrage spreads. If the evening peak price falls from $200/MWh to $100/MWh while the midday trough rises from -$20/MWh to $20/MWh, the spread halves. This is exactly what's happening — and it affects all batteries simultaneously, regardless of trading sophistication.

notes.title: Negative Prices — Batteries Are Absorbing Midday Oversupply

sections.heading: The Trend
sections.body: Q4 2025 saw a record 31% of all NEM intervals at negative prices — SA hit 48.4% negative, VIC 43.1%. But Q1 2026 showed improvement: 2,973 negative 30-minute periods, down 53% from Q4 2025 and 17% below Q1 2025.

sections.heading: Battery Effect
sections.body: The improvement is partly because battery charging absorbs midday oversupply, reducing negative price frequency. Average battery charging increased by 872 MW in Q1 2026 vs prior year. Batteries are essentially performing the grid-stabilisation function that justifies their deployment — even as it reduces the price volatility that drives their revenue.

sections.heading: The Paradox
sections.body: Batteries solving the negative price problem is a societal good but a commercial headwind. Fewer negative prices means less value in charging cheaply. This is the fundamental tension: batteries are essential infrastructure that erodes its own commercial viability through success.

notes.title: Renewables Hit Record 46.5% of NEM Supply — Instantaneous Record 78.6%

sections.heading: Quarterly Record
sections.body: Renewables supplied 46.5% of total NEM generation in Q1 2026 — the highest Q1 on record. Separately, the NEM hit an instantaneous renewable share of 78.6% in September 2025. Grid-scale and distributed solar both set quarterly generation records.

sections.heading: What's Being Displaced
sections.body: Coal generation declined 4.4% in Q1 2026. Gas generation fell to its lowest level since 1999. Rooftop solar continues growing at 8.1% YoY. The transition is no longer aspirational — it's happening at a pace that is reshaping wholesale market dynamics faster than most forecasts anticipated.

notes.title: Eraring Battery — The Coal-BESS Portfolio Play

sections.heading: What It Is
sections.body: Origin Energy's Eraring Battery Stage 1 (460 MW / 1,770 MWh) commenced operations in December 2025, adjacent to the 2.88 GW Eraring coal plant — the NEM's largest generator. It's the largest battery in the southern hemisphere. Total planned: 700 MW / 3,180 MWh across four stages by 2027. Wartsila battery systems.

sections.heading: The Portfolio Advantage
sections.body: Eraring Battery benefits from coal-BESS portfolio optimisation that standalone batteries cannot replicate. Origin can co-optimise coal dispatch and battery charge/discharge to maximise total portfolio value — charging from their own coal generation when it's cheap, discharging when coal ramps down. This is a structural advantage that will persist until Eraring coal closes (late 2027, possibly 1-2 units to early 2029).

sections.heading: Post-Coal Question
sections.body: When Eraring coal retires, the battery loses its portfolio effect. But it gains something else: Eraring's retirement removes 2.88 GW of baseload from NSW, which should restore significant price volatility — potentially the most important single event for NSW battery economics in the next 5 years.

notes.title: Waratah Super Battery — 850 MW, SIPS Contract, Partial Operations

sections.heading: Status
sections.body: The Waratah Super Battery (850 MW / 1,680 MWh, Akaysha Energy / BlackRock) began partial operations on 5 August 2025, with the first 350 MW / 750 MWh online. Full commissioning is expected during 2026. It is currently the world's most powerful battery by MW rating. Powin battery systems.

sections.heading: Revenue Model
sections.body: Unlike most NEM batteries, Waratah has a System Integrity Protection Scheme (SIPS) contract — providing grid security services to NSW that are not dependent on wholesale market arbitrage. This provides a contracted revenue floor that insulates it from the cannibalisation affecting merchant batteries.

sections.heading: Context
sections.body: Waratah was put on standby to prevent blackouts when four big coal units went offline. This is exactly the use case that justifies contracted system security batteries — their value is in reliability, not merchant revenue. The SIPS model may become more common as merchant returns compress.

notes.title: Behind-the-Meter Batteries — 219,000 Systems Installed Since Federal Subsidy

sections.heading: Scale
sections.body: Since the July 2025 federal battery subsidy launched, 219,000 behind-the-meter battery systems (4.7 GWh) have been installed across Australia. WattClarity asks: 'reasonable to ask when we'll start seeing obvious signs that behind-the-meter storage is eating into the market's dinner.'

sections.heading: Impact on Grid-Scale
sections.body: Behind-the-meter batteries reduce household grid demand during evening peaks — the same peaks that grid-scale batteries target for discharge revenue. At 4.7 GWh and growing, behind-the-meter storage is approaching the scale where it could materially reduce evening peak demand, further compressing the arbitrage spread available to grid-scale operators.

notes.title: Senate Estimates — CIS Construction Progress Update Expected

sections.heading: Upcoming Hearings
sections.body: The next round of Senate Estimates budget hearings is expected to include questions on CIS construction progress. Key areas to watch: how many CIS-contracted projects have reached financial close, construction start rates by tender round, and whether any contracted projects have been terminated or restructured.

sections.heading: Tracking Approach
sections.body: Monitor Hansard transcripts from the Environment and Communications Legislation Committee and the Economics Legislation Committee for ARENA/CEFC/CIS questioning. Key officials: DCCEEW Secretary, ARENA CEO, CEFC CEO.

notes.title: REZ Access Right Congestion — CWO and South-West NSW

sections.heading: Current State
sections.body: Central-West Orana has 13 access rights granted across 7,151 MW of allocated capacity, with a committed share of around 24%. South-West REZ has 6 access rights. Both zones face transmission congestion that limits the ability of new projects to secure firm access.

sections.heading: Implications for BESS
sections.body: Projects in these REZs without access rights face curtailment risk and should model revenue with a curtailment haircut. The Orana BESS (415 MW, Akaysha) is located in CWO and has CIS + EnergyAustralia offtake to mitigate. But merchant batteries in congested REZs face a double penalty: spread compression from cannibalisation AND curtailment from congestion.

notes.title: Coal Retirements 2027-2028 — The Key Variable for Battery Revenue Recovery

sections.heading: Scheduled Closures
sections.body: Eraring (2.88 GW, NSW) is set to close late 2027 — possibly keeping 1-2 units to early 2029. Yallourn (1.48 GW, VIC) is scheduled for mid-2028. Callide B (700 MW, QLD) in 2028. Combined: over 5 GW of baseload exiting the NEM within 2-3 years.

sections.heading: Revenue Implications
sections.body: Coal retirements remove baseload generation that currently suppresses evening and overnight prices. With coal gone, gas peakers and batteries become the only dispatchable supply during low-wind/low-solar periods. This should restore price volatility — the lifeblood of battery revenue. Eraring's closure alone could add $20-30/MWh to NSW evening peak prices.

sections.heading: Timing Gap
sections.body: The problem: 2026-2027 may be the worst period for battery revenue — maximum fleet deployment before coal retirements take effect. Batteries that can survive this trough with contracted revenue will benefit from the volatility rebound. Those relying purely on merchant revenue during the trough face severe stress.

notes.title: Market Price Cap Rises to $23,200/MWh from July 2026

sections.heading: The Change
sections.body: The NEM Market Price Cap increases from $20,300/MWh to $23,200/MWh effective 1 July 2026. This raises the theoretical upside potential during scarcity events — a single 5-minute interval at the new cap is worth 14% more.

sections.heading: Practical Impact
sections.body: Cap events are rare but outsized in revenue impact. A battery that captures even a few hours of cap pricing per year can earn more than months of regular arbitrage. The higher cap benefits batteries with high availability and fast response — particularly those with AEMO direction immunity. But it also increases the cost of missing a cap event (opportunity cost of being curtailed, constrained, or in the wrong charge state).

notes.title: NSW Warned: 75% of 56 GWh Storage Requirement for 2030 Not at FID

sections.heading: The Gap
sections.body: New South Wales has been warned that 75% of its 56 GWh storage requirement for 2030 has not yet reached financial investment decision (FID). This creates a significant policy risk: NSW needs storage for grid security as coal retires, but revenue cannibalisation is undermining the merchant investment case.

sections.heading: Policy Response
sections.body: This is the core tension driving LTESA Round 8 and CIS Tender 8 — government must underwrite battery investment because the merchant market alone cannot justify the build rate required. The question is whether contracted volumes will be sufficient, and whether contract terms can bridge the revenue trough until coal retirements restore volatility.

notes.title: AEMO 2026 ISP Expands BESS Target to 24 GW by 2030 — 9 GW Higher Than 2024

sections.heading: The Revised Target
sections.body: AEMO's 2026 draft Integrated System Plan targets 24 GW of grid-scale battery storage by 2030 — approximately 9 GW higher than the 2024 ISP projection. This reflects both the accelerated pace of deployment and the increased recognition of batteries as essential grid infrastructure.

sections.heading: What Drives the Increase
sections.body: Three factors: (1) Data centre demand growth projected at 4x by 2030 — the single largest new demand driver; (2) Accelerated coal retirement timelines creating earlier supply gaps; (3) Behind-the-meter solar and battery growth reducing the effectiveness of traditional supply-side solutions. The ISP essentially says: the NEM needs batteries even more than we thought, even though they're earning less than we expected.
